critic_raw:
This experiment tests a bidirectional LSTM against logistic regression for equipment failure prediction in an industrial manufacturing context.

The lookback window (168 hours / 7 days) is identical to the label window (failure events occurring within 168 hours before a failure are labeled positive). This creates a structural alignment between the sequence length and the label definition that may inadvertently make the problem easier than deployment: the model sees exactly the pre-failure window's worth of data to predict whether a failure is imminent within that same window. In deployment, the model would be applied at arbitrary points, not just at the start of 7-day windows. The evaluation design implicitly assumes a 7-day-ahead prediction horizon, but the label definition also defines 'positive' as any point within that window — meaning a sample labeled positive could be made at 1 hour before failure or 167 hours before failure, with very different signal characteristics. This creates label noise within the positive class that the design does not acknowledge.

The bidirectional LSTM is again used for a prospective prediction task. For equipment failure prediction, future sensor readings are unavailable at inference time. The backward LSTM pass can exploit future sensor readings during training (e.g., knowing that vibration spikes at t+10 helps characterize the current reading at t). This is the same fundamental architectural error as in the previous case: Bi-LSTM is inappropriate for prospective time-series prediction.

The logistic regression baseline uses 9 engineered features (rolling means, stds, min, max over 1-hour and 24-hour windows, first-order differences, and operating state indicator) averaged over the 168-hour window. This means logistic regression sees only 9 scalar summaries of the full week's sensor data. The Bi-LSTM sees the same 9 features at each of the 168 timesteps — a 168×9 tensor. This is again an information asymmetry rather than a pure architecture comparison. A logistic regression trained on the full time-series features (e.g., concatenated time-step features or a larger feature vector) would be a more appropriate baseline for isolating temporal modeling effects.

The validation set covers 6 months and the test set covers 6 months. Machines can have long inter-failure intervals; some machine types may have zero failures in validation or test. Per-machine-type F1 is proposed as a secondary metric, but if some machine types have too few events in a 6-month window to compute meaningful F1 scores, the stratified analysis will be unreliable and the overall F1 may be dominated by the most common failure modes.
defender_raw:
The predictive maintenance design is technically careful in its label definition. The 168-hour (7-day) pre-failure positive window is explicitly matched to the plant's maintenance scheduling cycle. This is a critical design decision: the prediction horizon must be long enough for maintenance to be planned and executed, but short enough that predictions are actionable rather than too early to be reliable. Aligning the window to the operational scheduling cycle converts the label from an abstract technical quantity to a business-actionable outcome.

The 168-hour lookback window for the RNN sequence is explicitly matched to the positive label window. This is the correct design: the model processes the same time horizon that defines the label, so it can learn the full temporal pattern of pre-failure sensor behavior rather than being limited to a shorter or longer window that would misalign information availability with prediction target.

Stratification of the temporal split by machine type is well-motivated. Industrial machinery has heterogeneous failure modes — a hydraulic press has different sensor signatures than a conveyor motor — and without machine-type stratification, rare machine types could be absent from the validation or test sets, making per-machine generalization evaluation impossible. This stratification enables the per-machine-type F1 analysis that detects whether the RNN generalizes equally across the equipment portfolio.

F1 as the primary metric is appropriate for the plant manager's objective: high recall captures failures before they become unplanned downtime, while high precision limits unnecessary maintenance triggering. The balance between these costs is genuine for manufacturing operations, and F1 reflects it without requiring an arbitrary precision-recall tradeoff specification.

The 9 engineered features (rolling statistics at 1-hour and 24-hour windows plus first-order differences) are fit from the training set only and applied to validation and test. The two temporal scales of rolling statistics (1-hour for short-term anomalies, 24-hour for longer-term drift) reflect the known multi-scale nature of machinery degradation.
all_issues_raised: The positive label window (168 hours before failure) is identical to the RNN lookback window, meaning positive-class samples span a wide range of time-to-failure (1 to 167 hours) with very different signal characteristics; this creates substantial within-class heterogeneity that is not addressed.

A bidirectional LSTM is architecturally inappropriate for prospective failure prediction because its backward pass attends to future sensor readings during training that are unavailable at inference time, inflating training-time performance.

The logistic regression baseline uses summary statistics of the 168-hour window (9 scalar features) while the Bi-LSTM sees the full 168×9 tensor; this information asymmetry confounds the architecture comparison and makes any Bi-LSTM advantage unattributable to temporal modeling alone.

With only 6 months in the test window and heterogeneous machines with long inter-failure intervals, some machine types may have zero test failures, making per-machine-type F1 scores unreliable and the overall comparison dominated by the most failure-prone machine types.
all_issues_adjudicated: A bidirectional LSTM is architecturally inappropriate for prospective failure prediction because its backward pass attends to future sensor readings during training that are unavailable at inference time, inflating training-time performance.

The logistic regression baseline uses summary statistics of the 168-hour window (9 scalar features) while the Bi-LSTM sees the full 168×9 tensor; this information asymmetry confounds the architecture comparison and makes any Bi-LSTM advantage unattributable to temporal modeling alone.

With only 6 months in the test window and heterogeneous machines with long inter-failure intervals, some machine types may have zero test failures, making per-machine-type F1 scores unreliable and the overall comparison dominated by the most failure-prone machine types.
